steps.title: Estimate the slope from the data trend
steps.content:
The scatterplot shows data ranging from high jump height $x \approx 1$ to 5 feet and long jump distance $y \approx 6$ to 15 feet.

Notice that as $x$ increases by 4 units (from 1 to 5), $y$ increases by approximately 9 units (from about 6 to 15).
$$\text{Approximate slope} \approx \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \approx \frac{15 - 6}{5 - 1} = \frac{9}{4} \approx 2.25$$
This tells us the best-fit line must be fairly steep — much steeper than a slope of 1 or less.

steps.title: Compare the given slope options
steps.content:
The four equations offer only two slope choices: 0.82 or 3.30.
  • Slope = 0.82 (choices A and B): Over a run of 4 units, this produces $0.82 \times 4 \approx 3.3$ units of rise — far too shallow compared to the observed ~9 unit rise.
  • Slope = 3.30 (choices C and D): Over a run of 4 units, this produces $3.30 \times 4 = 13.2$ units of rise — much closer to the observed ~9 unit rise.
Choices A and B are eliminated.

steps.title: Compare y-intercepts between C and D
steps.content:
Both C and D have slope 3.30 but differ only in the y-intercept:
  • Choice C: $y = 3.30x + 0.82$ (y-intercept = $+0.82$)
  • Choice D: $y = 3.30x - 3.30$ (y-intercept = $-3.30$)
Test at $x = 1$, where the scatterplot shows $y \approx 6$:
$$\text{Choice C: } y = 3.30(1) + 0.82 = 4.12$$
$$\text{Choice D: } y = 3.30(1) - 3.30 = 0$$
Choice C's prediction of 4.12 is much closer to the observed value of 6 than Choice D's prediction of 0. Checking additional points (like $x = 4$, where data ranges 13–17) confirms that C passes consistently closer to the data cluster than D.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
